replies.msg: 但如果有99個黑人，佢將一個黑人認錯做白人既機會，仲大過佢將個白人認岩做白人喎。

Law of total probability
Let event A = witness is correct, A^C = witness is incorrect
Event B = White person committed the crime
P(B) = P(B|A)·P(A) + P(B|A^C)·P(A^C) = 1·0.9 + 0·0.1 = 0.9
In other words, proportion of two races on the ship doesn't matter. Witness said is White, and he is 90% correct, so the culprit is white

One maybe tempted to find P(B|A) using Bayes' formula, but this is not what is being asked (if OP is sober).
One may also be tempted to calculate sth like this
Let
A = the culprit is black
B = identified as black
P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B) = (0.2 x 0.85) / (0.2 x 0.85 + 0.8 x 0.15) = 0.586

As someone did last year, but there is an obvious flaw: P(identified as black) = P(B) is 1, because witness already stated this fact. And the approach of looking for P(A|B) thing is not responding to the question.
Please enlighten me if I am wrong

Of course witness already stated the fact but this particular event do not occur 100%. If what you said is true then P(B) will always be 1 so P(A|B) = P(A and B).
Example: Filp two coins, Given the second one is head, what's the chance for both of them are head?
answer is P(both head | 2nd head) = P(both head and 2nd head) / P(2nd head)
P(2nd head) is not 1 even though witness already stated this fact.
